walkthrough.title: Place the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Scythe set up. First, unfold the large game board and place it in the center of the table. This is a gorgeous hex-based map of an alternate-history 1920s Eastern Europa. You will see different terrain types like forests, mountains, farms, tundra, and villages. Along the edges you will find the Triumph Track at the top, the Popularity Track on the left, and the Power Track on the right.

walkthrough.title: Place Encounter Tokens
walkthrough.text: Find the round encounter tokens with the question mark symbol. Place one encounter token face up on each territory on the board that has an encounter icon printed on it. There are typically eleven encounter spots. These tokens stay on the board until a player's character lands on them and resolves the encounter.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Supply
walkthrough.text: Now let's organize the shared supply. Place the resource tokens near the board, sorted by type. There are four kinds: food, wood, metal, and oil, with twenty of each. Stack the cardboard coins by denomination near the board as well. Set the multiplier tokens nearby for endgame scoring.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Card Decks
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the combat cards and place them face down as a draw pile near the board. Shuffle the encounter cards and place them in their own face-down deck. Shuffle the objective cards and place them in a separate deck. These three decks should be within easy reach of all players.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Factory Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the Factory cards. Shuffle them and deal a number of cards face down equal to the number of players plus one. For example, in a three-player game, place four Factory cards face down. Return the rest to the box without looking at them. Place the dealt cards face down near the Factory space on the board.

walkthrough.title: Choose the Structure Bonus
walkthrough.text: Find the six structure bonus tiles. Randomly select one and place it face up near the bottom of the Popularity Track on the board. Return the other five to the box. This tile determines the bonus scoring condition for structures at the end of the game.

walkthrough.title: Deal Faction Mats
walkthrough.text: Now let's set up each player. Randomly deal one faction mat to each player. Each faction mat shows a unique faction with its own special ability, starting power level, starting combat cards, and starting coins. The five factions are Rusviet, Crimea, Nordic, Polania, and Saxony. Return any undealt faction mats to the box.

walkthrough.title: Deal Player Mats
walkthrough.text: Separately from the faction mats, randomly deal one player mat to each player. These numbered mats determine your action layout and starting popularity. Each player mat has a different arrangement of top-row and bottom-row actions, which creates a unique strategic puzzle for every faction and player mat combination.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Your Faction Mat
walkthrough.text: Look at your faction mat. It shows your starting values. Place your power token on the Power Track at the number shown on your faction mat. Draw the number of combat cards indicated. Take the number of coins shown. Place your four mech miniatures on the four mech spaces on your faction mat. Place your six star tokens on the six star spaces.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Your Player Mat
walkthrough.text: Place your popularity token on the Popularity Track at the starting value shown on your player mat. Draw the number of objective cards indicated. Place all six technology cubes on the top-row spaces that have a black-square marker. Place your four structure tokens on the matching structure boxes. Place your four recruit tokens on the circular recruit spaces. Place your six reserve workers on the Produce action rectangles.

walkthrough.title: Place Units on the Board
walkthrough.text: Find your character miniature and place it on your faction's home base on the board. Each faction has a designated starting corner. Then place one worker on each of the two territories adjacent to your home base that are connected by land. These two workers start producing for you right away. Keep your action token beside your player mat.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: The player with the lowest-numbered player mat goes first. Play proceeds clockwise from there. Take a moment to look at your faction ability, your player mat layout, and your objective cards. You do not have to share your objectives with anyone. Setup is complete. Let's learn the rules.

summary.title: Place the Game Board
bullets: Unfold board centrally — hex map of Eastern Europa
Key tracks: Triumph (top), Popularity (left), Power (right)

bullets: 1 encounter token on each encounter-marked territory (~11 spots)
Stay on board until character resolves them

Stack coins by denomination near board
Multiplier tokens nearby for endgame

bullets: Shuffle combat cards → face-down draw pile
Shuffle encounter cards → separate deck
Shuffle objective cards → separate deck

bullets: Shuffle Factory cards → deal (players + 1) face down

Return extras to box unseen

Determines bonus scoring for structures at game end

Each has unique ability, starting power/cards/coins

Different mat = different strategic puzzle

Draw starting combat cards, take starting coins
Place 4 mechs + 6 stars on faction mat spaces

summary.title: Set Up Your Player Mat
bullets: Popularity token → Popularity Track at mat starting value
Draw starting objective cards
Place 6 tech cubes, 4 structures, 4 recruits, 6 workers on mat

summary.title: Place Units on the Board
bullets: Character miniature → home base on board
1 worker on each of 2 adjacent land territories
Action token beside player mat

summary.title: Determine First Player
bullets: Lowest-numbered player mat goes first
Play proceeds clockwise
Review faction ability, player mat layout, objectives

walkthrough.title: The Goal of Scythe
walkthrough.text: The goal is to have the most coins at the end of the game. You earn coins during play and during endgame scoring. The game ends immediately when any player places their sixth star on the Triumph Track. Stars represent achievements like completing all upgrades, deploying all mechs, winning combat, or reaching high popularity. But stars alone do not win. Your final score depends on coins, which come from stars, territories you control, resources you hold, and a structure bonus.

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you do one thing: choose one section of your player mat. Each section is a column with a top-row action and a bottom-row action. You may do the top action, the bottom action, both, or neither. The one rule is that you cannot choose the same section you chose on your previous turn. Move your action token to the new section to remember.

walkthrough.title: Top-Row Actions Overview
walkthrough.text: Your player mat has four top-row actions. Move lets you move your units across the map. Produce generates resources and workers on territories where you have workers. Trade lets you pay coins for resources or popularity. And Bolster lets you gain power or draw combat cards. The exact values depend on your player mat, and they improve as you upgrade.

walkthrough.title: Bottom-Row Actions Overview
walkthrough.text: Each top-row action is paired with a bottom-row action in the same column. The four bottom-row actions are Upgrade, Deploy, Build, and Enlist. Upgrade costs oil and improves your top-row actions while reducing bottom-row costs. Deploy costs metal and places a mech on the board. Build costs wood and places a structure. Enlist costs food and recruits an ally. Bottom-row actions are more expensive but earn you stars and permanent advantages.

walkthrough.title: Move Action
walkthrough.text: When you take the Move action, you may move up to two different units one territory each. Units are your character, mechs, and workers. Characters and mechs can move into territories occupied by opponents, which triggers combat. Workers can move but cannot initiate combat. You can also pick up and carry resources with your character or mechs as they move. Resources stay on the territory they are on unless you physically move them.

walkthrough.title: Rivers and Movement Restrictions
walkthrough.text: Rivers divide territories on the board, and most units cannot cross rivers unless they have a specific ability. Your faction's Riverwalk card shows which terrain types your character and mechs can cross rivers between. Workers can never cross rivers on their own. Deploying mechs unlocks movement abilities for your entire faction, which we will cover in a moment.

walkthrough.title: Produce Action
walkthrough.text: The Produce action generates resources on territories where you have workers. Each territory type produces a specific resource: farms produce food, forests produce wood, mountains produce metal, tundra produces oil, and villages produce workers instead of resources. You choose up to two territories to produce on. Each worker on a chosen territory produces one unit of that territory's resource. The more workers on a territory, the more it produces.

walkthrough.title: Trade Action
walkthrough.text: The Trade action lets you spend one coin to gain any two resources of your choice, placed on any territory you control with at least one worker. Alternatively, you can spend one coin to gain one point of popularity on the Popularity Track. Trade is useful when you need a specific resource type quickly without spending multiple turns producing it.

walkthrough.title: Bolster Action
walkthrough.text: The Bolster action lets you gain power on the Power Track or draw a combat card. Your player mat shows the specific amounts. Power is spent during combat and also earns you a star if you reach sixteen. Combat cards add secret power to combat. Bolstering early builds your military deterrent and prepares you for key battles.

walkthrough.title: Upgrade Action
walkthrough.text: Upgrade is a bottom-row action that costs oil. When you upgrade, you move one technology cube from a top-row action to a bottom-row action. The top-row action improves because it gains a bonus icon. The bottom-row action becomes cheaper because it loses a cost icon. Completing all six upgrades earns a star. Upgrades create a compounding advantage over the whole game.

walkthrough.title: Deploy Action
walkthrough.text: Deploy costs metal and places one of your four mech miniatures from your faction mat onto any territory you control that has at least one worker. When you deploy a mech, you unlock its ability for your entire faction. Mech abilities include things like crossing rivers, moving faster, or gaining combat advantages. Deploying all four mechs earns a star.

walkthrough.title: Build Action
walkthrough.text: Build costs wood and places one of your four structures onto any territory you control that has at least one worker. Each structure provides a unique ongoing benefit. The Mill produces resources when you produce on adjacent territories. The Monument gives you popularity when you bolster. The Mine acts as a tunnel connecting to other mines and the Factory. The Armory gives you power when you trade. Building all four earns a star, plus structures score with the structure bonus tile at game end.

walkthrough.title: Enlist Action
walkthrough.text: Enlist costs food and removes one of your four recruit tokens from your player mat. When you remove a recruit, you gain a one-time bonus of coins, power, popularity, or combat cards. You also gain an ongoing recruit bonus: whenever you or either adjacent player takes the corresponding bottom-row action, you gain the recruit benefit. Enlisting all four recruits earns a star.

walkthrough.title: Combat
walkthrough.text: This happens when your character or mech moves into a territory occupied by an opponent's character or mech. Workers do not fight. Both players secretly select power to spend on their power dials, from zero up to their current power. Then both may add one combat card per character or mech in the battle. Reveal simultaneously. Higher total wins. The winner stays on the territory. The loser retreats all their units and resources back to their home base.

walkthrough.title: Combat Stars and Consequences
walkthrough.text: The winner of combat places a star on the Triumph Track, up to a maximum of two combat stars per player. If the winner forces enemy workers to retreat, the winner loses one popularity for each displaced worker. This is important because popularity directly multiplies your endgame score. Winning a fight that displaces three workers costs you three popularity, which can be devastating.

walkthrough.title: Encounters
walkthrough.text: When your character moves onto a territory with an encounter token, you resolve the encounter. Draw the top encounter card. It presents three options, each offering different rewards like resources, popularity, power, or combat cards. Choose one option, gain the reward, and discard the card and the token. Encounters are free benefits that reward you for exploring the map.

walkthrough.title: The Factory
walkthrough.text: This is the large central territory on the board. The first time your character ends movement on the Factory, you may look at all the face-down Factory cards and choose one to keep. This card becomes a fifth action section on your player mat, giving you an extra action option for the rest of the game. Each player may only take one Factory card per game. At endgame, the Factory counts as three territories for scoring.

walkthrough.title: Objectives
walkthrough.text: Each player has secret objective cards. To complete an objective, you must meet the condition described on the card on your turn. When you do, reveal it and place a star on the Triumph Track. You can only score one objective star per game, so if you have multiple objectives, choose the one that fits your current game state best.

walkthrough.title: The Nine Achievement Stars
walkthrough.text: There are nine ways to earn stars. Complete all upgrades. Deploy all four mechs. Build all four structures. Enlist all four recruits. Have all eight workers on the board. Reveal a completed objective. Win combat up to two times. Reach eighteen popularity. Reach sixteen power. You only need six to end the game. Each star also scores you coins at game end.

walkthrough.title: Game End Trigger
walkthrough.text: The game ends immediately when any player places their sixth star. It does not matter whose turn it is. That player finishes their current action, and then scoring begins. The player who triggered the end does not necessarily win. Victory is determined by final coin totals.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Scoring
walkthrough.text: Final scoring has four parts. First, keep all coins you earned during the game. Second, score coins for your stars, territories controlled, and every two resources you control. The number of coins you earn for each depends on your popularity tier: the higher your popularity, the more each star, territory, and resource pair is worth. Third, add the structure bonus if applicable. The player with the most total coins wins.

walkthrough.title: Popularity Tiers
walkthrough.text: The Popularity Track has three tiers that determine your endgame multiplier. Tier one is zero to three popularity, and it gives the lowest coin values. Tier two is four to six popularity, which roughly doubles the payout. Tier three is seven to eighteen popularity, giving the highest values. Moving from tier two to tier three can be worth twenty or more coins in endgame scoring. Popularity is one of the most important resources in the game.

walkthrough.title: Territory Control
walkthrough.text: You control a territory if you have at least one unit on it, or if you have a structure on it with no enemy units present. Home bases do not count as territories. The Factory counts as three territories. At game end, you want to be spread across as many territories as possible while maintaining high popularity to maximize each territory's coin value.

walkthrough.title: Winning and Tiebreakers
walkthrough.text: The player with the most coins wins Scythe. A typical winning score is around seventy-five coins. If tied, the first tiebreaker is most territories controlled. Then most resources controlled. Then most stars placed. Scythe rewards balanced play across economy, military, and popularity rather than excelling in just one area.

summary.title: The Goal of Scythe
bullets: Most coins at game end wins
Game ends when any player places 6th star
Coins from stars + territories + resources, multiplied by popularity

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Tutorial
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through the opening turns of a three-player game. Player 1 plays the Nordic faction, Player 2 plays Rusviet, and Player 3 plays Crimea. Each player will demonstrate different strategies and make some common mistakes along the way. Follow along even if you have a different player count or different factions.

walkthrough.title: Starting Positions
walkthrough.text: Each faction starts in their corner of the board with their character on the home base and two workers on adjacent territories. Nordic starts near lakes in the north. Rusviet starts in the forested east. Crimea starts in the steppes of the south. Each player also has different starting power, coins, and combat cards based on their faction mat.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Produce
walkthrough.text: They select their two territories with workers. One worker is on a forest, producing one wood. The other is on a mountain, producing one metal. Resources appear on the territories where they were produced, not on the player mat. Player 1 now has wood and metal sitting on the map ready to be used.

walkthrough.title: Why Producing First Is Smart
walkthrough.text: Producing on your first turn is one of the most common openings in Scythe. Resources fuel everything: bottom-row actions like upgrading, deploying, building, and enlisting all cost resources. Getting resources onto the board early means you can start investing in your engine sooner. The earlier you upgrade or deploy, the longer you benefit from the improvement.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Move
walkthrough.text: Characters are the only units that can resolve encounters, and encounter rewards are powerful free benefits. Player 2 also moves one worker from a village to an adjacent farm to set up food production for next turn.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: Bolster
walkthrough.text: Crimea has a faction ability that lets them spend combat cards as resources, making early bolstering doubly valuable. Player 3 is building military strength and economic flexibility at the same time.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Trade and Upgrade
walkthrough.text: They spend one coin to gain two oil tokens on a worker territory. Then they pay three oil for the bottom-row Upgrade action, moving one tech cube. The top-row Move action just improved, and the bottom-row Deploy action just got cheaper. That is a strong combo turn using both top and bottom actions.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Mistake: Repeating Dead Turns
walkthrough.text: Player 2 wants to move their character again to reach the encounter, but they chose Move last turn. They cannot repeat the same section. They are stuck choosing a different action first. Player 2 did not plan ahead and now must take an action they are not ready for, essentially wasting a turn. They bolster for a small power gain.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Plan Two Turns Ahead
walkthrough.text: The section-repeat restriction is the core puzzle of Scythe. You must always plan at least two turns ahead. Ask yourself: if I choose this section now, what will I choose next turn? A good two-turn sequence builds your engine efficiently. A bad one leaves you stuck with a wasted action. Experienced players plan a repeating loop of two or three sections that feed into each other.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: Produce and Enlist
walkthrough.text: They pay the food cost, remove a recruit token, and gain the one-time bonus of two coins. Now, whenever Player 3 or an adjacent player takes the Deploy action, Player 3 gains a recruit bonus. Enlisting early creates passive income for the rest of the game.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Produce Again
walkthrough.text: They now have enough metal to deploy a mech next turn. Notice how Player 1 is alternating between two sections, Produce and Trade/Upgrade, creating an efficient loop. Each turn builds directly toward the next move.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Move to Encounter
walkthrough.text: They draw an encounter card and choose the option that grants three food and one popularity. Encounters offer powerful one-time benefits, and routing your character toward them in the first few turns is almost always worth it.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Mistake: Spreading Too Thin
walkthrough.text: Player 3 gets excited about expanding and moves workers in three different directions on consecutive turns. Now their workers are scattered across four different territories, each one alone. Producing on any single territory only generates one resource. Meanwhile, Player 1 has two workers on the same mountain, producing two metal per Produce action. Player 3's expansion looks impressive on the map but their economy is crawling.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Concentrate Workers Early
walkthrough.text: Spreading workers thin early in Scythe is a common trap. Two workers on one territory produce twice as much as one worker on two territories, but the concentrated approach takes fewer actions. In the early game, focus workers on two or three key territories that produce the resources you need most. Expand later when you have mechs to protect your positions.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Deploy a Mech
walkthrough.text: The mech unlocks the Riverwalk ability, letting all of Player 1's characters and mechs cross rivers between specific terrain types. Suddenly Player 1 can reach territories that were previously blocked. This opens up the map significantly.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Mistake: Ignoring Popularity
walkthrough.text: By turn five, Player 2 has zero popularity gains. They have been focusing entirely on movement and resources. But popularity is the multiplier for endgame scoring. If Player 2 finishes in tier one with zero to three popularity, every star and territory is worth far fewer coins than a player in tier three. Player 2 is building a strong board position that will score poorly.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Track Popularity From Turn One
walkthrough.text: Popularity is the most commonly neglected resource in Scythe, and it is arguably the most important. The difference between tier one and tier three can be twenty or more coins at game end. Sprinkle popularity gains throughout the game using the Trade action, encounters, enlist bonuses, and the Monument structure. Do not wait until the end to climb the track.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: Combat Decision
walkthrough.text: Player 3's character approaches a territory with Player 1's lone worker and some resources. Player 3 considers attacking to steal the territory and resources, but there are two workers present. If Player 3 wins, those workers retreat and Player 3 loses two popularity. Player 3 wisely decides to move elsewhere, avoiding the popularity hit.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game Checkpoint
walkthrough.text: Let's check in on where everyone stands after five or six turns. Player 1 has a steady produce-upgrade loop going, one mech deployed, and is working toward a second. Player 2 is catching up after the wasted turn but has gained some encounter benefits. Player 3 has enlisted and is generating passive income but needs to consolidate workers. All three players are building toward different star paths.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Mistake: Bad End Timing
walkthrough.text: Let's fast forward. Player 2 races to six stars by winning two combats, completing upgrades, deploying all mechs, enlisting all recruits, and completing an objective. But Player 2 only has three popularity, placing them in tier one. Meanwhile, Player 1 is in tier three with five stars and controls eight territories. When the scores are tallied, Player 1 wins by fifteen coins despite having fewer stars. Player 2 triggered the end too early.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Stars Do Not Equal Victory
walkthrough.text: Placing the sixth star ends the game, but it does not mean you win. Stars are just one of several scoring categories. If your popularity is low or you control few territories, your stars will not convert to enough coins. Before racing to end the game, check your popularity tier, territory count, and resource holdings. Sometimes it is better to hold at five stars and spend a few turns improving your position before closing out.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen how to build an action loop, how encounters and mech deployments open the map, and how popularity drives endgame scoring. You have also learned three crucial lessons: plan two turns ahead to avoid dead actions, concentrate workers early for efficient production, and never neglect popularity.

walkthrough.title: Before You March
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for opening priorities and common mistakes to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to every action, star condition, and faction ability.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

Low popularity tier → all endgame scoring worth less

bullets: Tier 1 → Tier 3 difference = 20+ coins at game end
Gain popularity via Trade, encounters, enlists, Monument
Sprinkle popularity throughout — don't wait until end

bullets: Could attack territory with 2 enemy workers + resources
Winning → lose 2 popularity for displaced workers
Wisely avoids the fight — popularity too valuable

bullets: Player 1: steady produce-upgrade loop, 1 mech deployed

summary.title: Player 2's Mistake: Bad End Timing
bullets: Races to 6 stars but only 3 popularity (tier 1)
Player 1: 5 stars, tier 3 popularity, 8 territories

bullets: 6th star ends game but doesn't guarantee winning

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: Covered action loops, encounters, mech deployment, popularity
3 key lessons: plan 2 turns ahead, concentrate workers, track popularity
You're ready for a full game!

summary.title: Before You March
bullets: Check out General Tips → opening priorities and common mistakes
Reference the Appendix → actions, stars, faction abilities
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Build a Repeatable Action Loop
walkthrough.text: The most important skill in Scythe is building an efficient action loop. Because you cannot repeat the same section two turns in a row, you need at least two sections that feed into each other. A classic loop is Produce on one turn and Upgrade or Deploy on the next. The produce generates resources, and the bottom-row action spends them. When your loop runs smoothly, every turn advances your engine.

walkthrough.title: Upgrade Early and Often
walkthrough.text: Upgrades are the best long-term investment in Scythe. Each upgrade makes one top-row action stronger and one bottom-row action cheaper. The benefits compound over the entire game. Getting two or three upgrades in the first five turns gives you a meaningful advantage on every subsequent turn. Prioritize the upgrade that improves your most-used top-row action and reduces the cost of your most-needed bottom-row action.

walkthrough.title: Expand Your Worker Base
walkthrough.text: Workers are the foundation of your economy. More workers on productive territories means more resources per Produce action. Aim to get at least four workers on the board within the first few turns. Place them on territories that produce the resources you need most. Remember, deploying all eight workers earns a star, so worker expansion is both economically productive and a path to victory.

walkthrough.title: Protect Your Resources
walkthrough.text: Resources sit on the map, not in your hand. That means opponents can steal them by winning combat on your territories. Keep your resource stockpiles on territories you can defend, ideally behind rivers or near your mechs. Do not leave a pile of five oil sitting on an exposed frontier territory. If an opponent takes that territory in combat, they get all the resources too.

walkthrough.title: Popularity Is the Multiplier
walkthrough.text: New players often underestimate popularity. The difference between tier one and tier three can be worth twenty to thirty coins. Make small investments in popularity throughout the game rather than trying to climb the whole track at the end. The Trade action, encounters, the Monument structure, and enlist bonuses all provide popularity. Aim for at least tier two, and stretch for tier three if possible.

walkthrough.title: Use Encounters for Tempo
walkthrough.text: Encounter tokens give free resources, popularity, power, or other benefits. Routing your character toward encounters in the first three or four turns is almost always worthwhile. The rewards accelerate your development without costing resources. Plan your character's path at the start of the game to pick up one or two encounters on the way to the Factory.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Here are the biggest beginner traps. First, tunnel vision on one action type while neglecting others. Second, triggering the sixth star while behind in popularity and territories. Third, overfighting after you already have two combat stars. Fourth, leaving resources exposed on the frontier. Fifth, forgetting your secret objective until it is too late to pivot. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the most costly errors.

walkthrough.title: Read the Table
walkthrough.text: Scythe is a game of timing and positioning. Watch what your opponents are building toward. If someone is approaching six stars, you might need to rush your own scoring or block their final achievement. If no one has much military power, you might be able to expand aggressively with less risk. The best Scythe players adapt their plans based on what the table is doing, not just what their own engine wants.

walkthrough.title: Rush vs. Engine Strategy
walkthrough.text: The fundamental strategic choice in Scythe is whether to rush six stars quickly or build a strong economic engine for maximum coin conversion. A rush strategy places stars fast, often through combat, objectives, and completing one or two bottom-row tracks. An engine strategy invests heavily in upgrades, workers, and popularity, aiming for fewer stars but higher coin values per star. The right approach depends on your faction, player mat, and what opponents are doing.

walkthrough.title: Faction-Specific Openings
walkthrough.text: Each faction has a natural opening that plays to its strengths. Rusviet can repeat sections using their faction ability, enabling faster engine loops. Crimea can use combat cards as resources, making Bolster an economic action. Nordic can swim across lakes, giving early map access. Polania draws extra encounter options, maximizing exploration value. Saxony can earn unlimited combat stars, enabling an aggressive military approach. Lean into your faction's unique advantage from turn one.

walkthrough.title: Player Mat Optimization
walkthrough.text: Your player mat determines which action pairings are most efficient. Study the columns to find the strongest two-turn loop. Some mats pair Produce with Deploy, making a produce-deploy cycle very efficient. Others pair Trade with Enlist, enabling a coin-to-food-to-recruit pipeline. The best players identify their mat's natural loop during setup and structure their entire game plan around it. Upgrades should prioritize improving the actions in your primary loop.

walkthrough.title: Timing the Factory
walkthrough.text: The Factory card gives you a fifth action section, which is extremely powerful. But reaching the Factory costs several Move actions that could be spent on other things. The ideal timing is to pick up one or two encounters on the way to the Factory, arriving around turn six to eight. If the Factory is too far from your starting position, it might not be worth the detour. Evaluate the opportunity cost before committing to the trip.

walkthrough.title: Combat Timing and Deterrence
walkthrough.text: Combat in Scythe is about timing and deterrence, not constant fighting. You want to fight exactly twice for your two combat stars, and only when the territory gains and star value outweigh the power and popularity costs. Keep your power and combat cards visible enough to deter opponents from attacking your key territories. The threat of combat is often more valuable than combat itself. Save your big fights for moments when the territory, star, and resources are all worth the investment.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Positioning
walkthrough.text: In the last three or four turns, shift your focus from engine building to endgame positioning. Spread your units across as many territories as possible. Move resources to safe territories you will control at game end. Check your popularity tier and consider spending a turn on Trade for one more popularity point if it pushes you into the next tier. Count your opponent's stars. If someone is about to end the game, make sure you are positioned to score well when it happens.

walkthrough.title: The Sixth Star Decision
walkthrough.text: Placing the sixth star is one of the most important decisions in Scythe. Before you end the game, calculate your approximate score and compare it to what your opponents likely have. Count their stars, territories, visible resources, and popularity. If you are ahead, end it immediately. If you are close but behind, it may be better to hold at five stars and spend a turn or two improving your position while hoping opponents do not end it first.

walkthrough.title: Structure Bonus Optimization
walkthrough.text: The structure bonus tile selected during setup awards coins based on structure placement patterns. Common bonuses include structures in a row, structures on tunnels, or structures adjacent to encounters or lakes. Plan your building order to maximize this bonus. A well-placed set of four structures can be worth six to nine bonus coins, which often makes the difference in a close game.

walkthrough.title: Multi-Player Dynamics
walkthrough.text: In games with four or five players, table dynamics become critical. Players who attack early become targets. Players who appear weakest get left alone to build freely. Position yourself as a moderate threat, strong enough that attacking you is costly but not so strong that the table gangs up on you. Pay attention to which players are adjacent to you and manage those relationships. Sometimes trading popularity for peace is the smartest play.

walkthrough.title: Recruit Bonus Chains
walkthrough.text: Enlist bonuses trigger whenever you or either adjacent player takes the matching bottom-row action. In a full game, this can trigger many times. The best recruits to enlist first are the ones that match bottom-row actions your neighbors are likely to take frequently. If the player to your left is building structures rapidly, enlist the Build recruit to gain coins or power every time they build. Recruit chains can generate ten or more bonus resources over the course of a game.

Right choice depends on faction, mat, and opponents

entries.definition: Move up to the indicated number of different units one territory each. Characters and mechs can enter opponent territories to initiate combat. Workers move but do not fight. Units can carry resources when moving.

Choose up to the indicated number of territories where you have workers. Each worker on a chosen territory produces one resource matching the terrain type. Villages produce workers instead of resources.
Pay one coin to gain two resources of any type placed on a territory you control with a worker, or pay one coin to gain one popularity.
Gain the indicated amount of power on the Power Track, or draw the indicated number of combat cards from the combat deck.
Pay oil to move one technology cube from a top-row action (improving it) to a bottom-row action (reducing its cost). All six upgrades complete earns a star.
Pay metal to place one mech from your faction mat onto a territory you control with a worker. Unlocks the deployed mech's ability for your entire faction. All four mechs deployed earns a star.
Pay wood to place one structure from your player mat onto a territory you control with a worker. Each structure provides an ongoing benefit. All four structures built earns a star.

Pay food to remove one recruit token from your player mat. Gain a one-time bonus and an ongoing bonus that triggers when you or adjacent players take the matching bottom-row action. All four recruits enlisted earns a star.
Your faction's hero miniature. Can move, carry resources, initiate combat, resolve encounters, and claim one Factory card per game. Starts on home base.
Armored unit that can move, carry resources, initiate combat, and transport workers. Each deployed mech unlocks a faction-specific ability. Four per faction.

Civilian unit that enables production, deployment, and building on territories. Workers move but cannot initiate combat. If forced to retreat by combat, the winner loses popularity. Eight per faction.
The track at the top of the board where players place achievement stars. The game ends when any player places their sixth star. Stars score coins at endgame based on popularity tier.

Track on the left side of the board measuring public support. Three tiers determine endgame coin values. Tier 1 (0-3), Tier 2 (4-6), Tier 3 (7-18). Higher popularity means more coins per star, territory, and resource pair.
Track on the right side of the board measuring military strength. Power is spent in combat. Reaching 16 power earns a star.

Central territory on the board. The first time your character ends movement here, choose one Factory card to add as a fifth action section to your player mat. Counts as three territories for endgame scoring.
Your faction's starting territory. Does not count as a territory for endgame scoring. Units retreat here when they lose combat. You cannot normally move into opponents' home bases.
Token on specific territories. When your character moves there, draw an encounter card and choose one of three options for a free reward. Removed after resolution.

Triggered when a character or mech enters a territory occupied by an opponent's character or mech. Both players secretly select power to spend and may add combat cards. Higher total wins. Loser retreats all units and resources to home base.
Cards with power values (2-5) that add to your total in combat. Play one card per character or mech you have in the battle. Cards are discarded after use. Drawn via the Bolster action.
Secret dial used during combat to select how much power to spend. Both players reveal simultaneously. Spent power is deducted from the Power Track.
Star earned by winning combat. Each player can earn a maximum of two combat stars. Saxony faction exception: no combat star limit.
Secret card with a specific condition. Reveal on your turn when the condition is met to place a star. Only one objective star per game. Choose the most achievable objective if you hold multiple.
Resource produced on farm territories. Used to pay the Enlist bottom-row action cost.
Resource produced on forest territories. Used to pay the Build bottom-row action cost.
Resource produced on mountain territories. Used to pay the Deploy bottom-row action cost.
Resource produced on tundra territories. Used to pay the Upgrade bottom-row action cost.
The victory currency of Scythe. Earned during play and during endgame scoring. Coins are not resources and cannot be stolen in combat. Most coins at game end wins.
When you take the Produce action, the Mill's territory produces as if it had one additional worker, even if no workers are there.
When you take the Bolster action, gain one additional popularity.
Acts as a tunnel. Your character and mechs can move between the Mine and any other Mine or the Factory as if they were adjacent.
When you take the Trade action, gain one additional power.

Randomly selected during setup. Awards bonus coins at endgame based on how your four structures are placed relative to each other or the map. Examples: structures in a row, adjacent to tunnels, near lakes.
Faction-specific ability shown on the Riverwalk card. Defines which terrain pairs your character and mechs can cross rivers between. Workers cannot cross rivers.
Faction ability: may choose the same section on consecutive turns (ignoring the repeat restriction). Starting position in eastern forests.
Faction ability: may spend combat cards as if they were any resource type. Starting position in southern steppes.
Faction ability: workers may swim across rivers to adjacent territories. Starting position in northern lakes region.
Faction ability: when resolving encounters, may choose from one additional option (see two extra options). Starting position in western farms.
Faction ability: no limit on combat stars or objective stars (can earn more than two combat stars and score multiple objectives). Starting position in southwestern mountains.
Small cube placed on player mat during setup. Moving a cube from a top-row space to a bottom-row space is the Upgrade action. Six cubes total per player.
Round token placed on player mat recruit spaces during setup. Removing one is the Enlist action. Grants one-time and ongoing bonuses. Four per player.
Token placed on your player mat to mark which section you chose this turn. Prevents accidentally repeating the same section next turn.

At endgame, popularity determines coin value per scored item. Tier 1 (0-3 pop): 3/2/1 coins per star/territory/resource pair. Tier 2 (4-6): 4/3/2. Tier 3 (7-18): 5/4/3. Higher tiers earn substantially more.

You cannot choose the same player mat section on two consecutive turns. This is the core strategic constraint of Scythe, requiring players to plan multi-turn action sequences. Rusviet faction ignores this rule.
